_patch_meta.description: Dimension review patch — C08: 114 dimension updates (boldness, courage, devotion, endurance, generosity, gentleness, humility, patience, self-control); Regs 48 and 109 = 0 groups; 1 group already anchored (1108-001); 8 Session B findings; 1 Session D pointer; 5 Volitional/Capacity corrections

record.finding: parrēsia (G3954/3955) shows a tripartite inner-being structure: (1) access to God in prayer/worship (Spiritual/God-ward, 713-001), (2) apostolic proclamation against opposition (Character/Disposition + Spiritual/God-ward, 713-002, 714-001), (3) relational openness (Relational/Social, 713-003). Boldness is contextually differentiated — its nature shifts with its object and context. Session B should map how boldness-as-God-ward-access, boldness-as-prophetic-character, and boldness-as-relational-quality relate in the inner being.

operations.description: Session B pointer — boldness/parrēsia: tripartite contextual differentiation

record.finding: cha.zaq (H2388G) group 4923-002 ('Be strong and courageous' grounded in divine presence) is the paradigmatic biblical courage formula: courage is not self-generated but is a response to and product of divine accompaniment. The command and the promise of presence are structurally inseparable. Session B should examine cha.zaq's courage vocabulary as the primary inner-being theological claim of Reg 33: courage as relational-received-from-God, not as an autonomous character virtue.

operations.description: Session B pointer — courage/cha.zaq: courage as God-ward relational reception, not autonomous virtue

record.finding: Registry 46 (devotion) centres on haplotēs/haplous — singleness, undivided-ness, wholeness of inner orientation. The primary analytical claim is that devotion is not primarily an emotional state (fervour) but a structural condition of the inner being: the undivided attention of the whole person toward God. The distraction group (5034-001) names the failure mode: the inner being pulled and scattered. Session B should examine singleness-of-heart as a central inner-being category distinct from both the zeal vocabulary of C07 and the mere performance of worship.

operations.description: Session B pointer — devotion/haplotēs: singleness-of-heart as structural inner-being condition

record.finding: agathos (G0018) groups in Reg 65 frame the inner being's relationship to goodness in four registers: (1) goodness as character source (good heart — Character/Disposition, 881-002), (2) goodness as moral command (do good — Moral/Conscience, 881-003), (3) goodness as inner conflict (want but cannot — Volitional/Will, 881-005), (4) goodness as divine attribute (only God is good — Theological/Divine-Human, 881-001). Group 881-005 is analytically most significant — the gap between inner desire for good and capacity to perform it is the heart of Pauline anthropology. Session B should examine how these four registers of goodness interact and what they reveal about the inner being's relationship to moral reality.

operations.description: Session B pointer — generosity/agathos: four-register goodness structure (source, command, conflict, divine attribute)

record.finding: a.nah (H6031B) groups show affliction operating as an inner-being shaper in three modes: (1) affliction by others (1923-001 — experienced suffering), (2) affliction by God (1923-002 — purposive divine formation), (3) voluntary self-affliction (1923-003 — chosen submission). The same term covers all three, suggesting that the gentleness/meekness registry understands affliction as the formative mechanism of inner character. Session B should examine how gentleness is structurally connected to the affliction vocabulary — whether biblical gentleness is primarily the fruit of endured humbling.

operations.description: Session B pointer — gentleness/a.nah: affliction in three modes as inner-being formation mechanism

record.finding: Registry 80 (humility) shows the biblical vocabulary of humility operating primarily as Character/Disposition (8 groups) with significant Theological/Divine-Human presence (4 groups). Humility is understood primarily as a character quality while simultaneously being the object of divine regard and the fruit of divine action (God humbling the proud). Group 57-002 (false humility) is analytically significant — the outward performance of abasement without genuine inner transformation. Session B should examine whether biblical humility is something the person cultivates, something God works in the person, or whether these are two aspects of the same inner reality, and how false humility is identified.

operations.description: Session B pointer — humility: character virtue vs. divine gift; and false humility as inner-being failure

record.finding: Registry 116 (patience) shows a structural split: patience as human character quality (Character/Disposition, 7 groups) and patience as divine attribute (Theological/Divine-Human, 3 groups — 32-002, 33-002, 83-001). The divine patience groups show God's own inner disposition of forbearance modelled as the archetype of what believers are commanded to embody. Session B should examine whether biblical patience is explicitly presented as imitation of the divine disposition, and whether the vocabulary signals this connection.

operations.description: Session B pointer — patience: human character quality mirroring divine attribute

record.finding: Registry 142 (self-control) shows the self-governance vocabulary operating in three registers: (a) human self-control as character virtue (1106-001, 1113-001, 6203-001), (b) human volitional exercise of restraint (1107-001, 6208-001, 1109-001, 1111-001), (c) Spirit-gift of self-discipline as divine endowment (1112-001). The human effort and the divine gift coexist and the vocabulary does not sharply distinguish them. Session B should examine whether biblical self-control is primarily a virtue cultivated, a gift received, or an exercise of will — or whether all three are held in tension as aspects of the same inner-being governance.

operations.description: Session B pointer — self-control: three registers (character virtue, volitional exercise, Spirit-gift)

record.description: Groups 763-001 and 763-002 (tharseō — divine 'Take heart/be courageous' address) in Reg 33 mirror the 'Fear not' pattern from Reg 61 (ya.re) in C06. The divine address directly to the inner being producing courage where there was fear is a structured biblical motif crossing C06 and C08. Session D should examine the divine courage-address as a cross-cluster pattern: how God's direct speech to the inner being (commanding against fear, commanding toward courage) functions as a distinctive inner-being transformation mechanism.

operations.description: Session D pointer — divine courage-address (tharseō/Reg 33) mirrors divine fear-address (ya.re/Reg 61): cross-cluster pattern
